claims.claim_text: The Polymathic Studios fine-tuning disaster with eight GPUs, 100,000 images, an $85,000 bill, and cancelled contracts.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Consider the story of Polymathic Studios, a mid-sized digital design agency that attempted to fine-tune a full Stable Diffusion XL model for their enterprise clients. They spun up a dedicated cloud cluster with eight high-end GPUs, aiming to train the model on a proprietary catalog of 100,000 product images. By the end of the month, their compute bill exceeded $85,000, and the resulting model was so catastrophically overfitted that it could only generate blurry, artifact-ridden shapes. The financial impact was devastating, nearly bankrupting the division and forcing the cancellation of three major client contracts. This failure was a direct result of relying on full-parameter fine-tuning without understanding modern architectural efficiency.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Full-parameter fine-tuning of large diffusion models can become extremely expensive and can still fail if the data pipeline, regularization strategy, and objective are poorly chosen.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with named entities and precise operational and financial details.

claims.claim_text: Stanford Alpaca used PEFT to adapt a 7B model for roughly $600.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, second paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Contrast this catastrophic failure with the Alpaca project by Stanford researchers, who used Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques to successfully adapt a massive 7-billion parameter language model for roughly $600.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Contrast that with low-cost instruction-tuning efforts such as Stanford Alpaca, which showed that adapting a 7B language model could be done on a much smaller budget than full-model retraining.
claims.rationale: Stanford Alpaca is real, but this sentence incorrectly attributes the result to PEFT and overstates the exact setup.

claims.claim_text: U-Net was originally introduced for biomedical image segmentation.
claims.span_hint: section: The U-Net Architecture, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The original U-Net paper directly establishes the architecture's biomedical image segmentation origin.

claims.claim_text: DDPM sampling is formulated as a reverse Markov chain over many timesteps.
claims.span_hint: section: Schedulers: DDPM vs DDIM, DDPM subsection
claims.rationale: The DDPM paper is the primary source for the reverse Markov-chain formulation.

claims.claim_text: DDIM uses a non-Markovian path, and eta=0 gives deterministic DDIM while higher eta reintroduces stochasticity.
claims.span_hint: section: Schedulers: DDPM vs DDIM, DDIM subsection
claims.rationale: The DDIM scheduler docs explicitly describe the non-Markovian formulation and the eta behavior.

claims.claim_text: CLIP maps text and images into a shared embedding space.
claims.span_hint: section: Text Conditioning and CLIP, first paragraph
claims.rationale: The CLIP paper is the primary source for the joint text-image embedding formulation.

claims.claim_text: Latent diffusion models inject text embeddings into the U-Net with cross-attention.
claims.span_hint: section: Text Conditioning and CLIP, cross-attention paragraph
claims.rationale: The latent diffusion paper directly describes cross-attention conditioning for text-guided generation.

claims.claim_text: Classifier-free guidance trains conditional and unconditional paths jointly by dropping conditioning during training.
claims.span_hint: section: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG), first paragraph
claims.rationale: The classifier-free guidance paper directly introduces joint conditional and unconditional training.

claims.claim_text: Stable Diffusion uses latent diffusion with a VAE and CLIP text encoder to reduce compute and memory relative to pixel-space diffusion.
claims.span_hint: section: Stable Diffusion Architecture, first two paragraphs
claims.rationale: The Stable Diffusion overview explicitly states the latent diffusion setup and its compute/memory motivation.

claims.claim_text: LoRA freezes base weights and adds low-rank trainable matrices to reduce trainable parameters and memory use.
claims.span_hint: section: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: Enter LoRA, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The original LoRA paper directly makes this claim.

claims.claim_text: Dreambooth vs LoRA training-time comparison row.
claims.span_hint: section: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: Enter LoRA, comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Training time | Usually longer | Usually shorter |

claims.claim_text: Dreambooth vs LoRA model-size comparison row.
claims.span_hint: section: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: Enter LoRA, comparison table
claims.anchor_text: | Model size | Full copy (~5GB) | Adapter only (~10-100MB) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Model size | Full model copy | Much smaller adapter artifact |

claims.claim_text: Dreambooth vs LoRA combinability comparison row.
claims.span_hint: section: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: Enter LoRA, comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Combinability | More cumbersome to combine experimentally | Often easier to combine experimentally |
claims.rationale: This comparative claim is directionally plausible but unsupported as written.

claims.claim_text: The Getty-style recall story with $2.3 million in legal fees and settlements from watermark remnants.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, Getty Images vs AI Art
claims.anchor_text: A marketing director at a major consumer goods company received an urgent call. Their Q1 campaign, heavily featuring dozens of AI-generated product images, had been externally flagged: several generated images contained deeply subtle watermarks—unintended remnants of massive training data memorized inadvertently by the foundation diffusion model. The overall resulting cost was immense: $2.3 million scattered across intensive legal fees and immediate settlements. Thorough dataset verification prevents this.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams using image generators in marketing should check outputs and training-data provenance carefully, because memorized artifacts or watermark-like remnants can create legal and brand risk.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed legal/incident anecdote with precise financial impact.

claims.claim_text: The support-ticket avalanche story about a startup whose GPU cluster melted under viral load.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, The Support Ticket Avalanche
claims.anchor_text: A small tech startup's API was operating smoothly until a massive viral social media hit severely overloaded their primary endpoints. During an emergency post-mortem, they discovered their backend engineers had accidentally left inference steps bound to extreme defaults. Each image inherently took significantly too long to properly render. Their dedicated GPU cluster functionally melted under the extreme strain, stranding them with thousands of angry user support tickets and an exorbitant cloud bill.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Generation settings that are acceptable in demos can become operationally expensive in production, so inference step counts and scheduler choices need to be tuned against real traffic and latency budgets.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with implied operational specifics but no source.

claims.claim_text: The NSFW filter failure story with a 92% classifier, an 8% miss rate, viral screenshots, and app-store bans in two days.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, The NSFW Filter Failure
claims.anchor_text: An expanding educational platform deployed a relatively basic, unsophisticated NSFW classifier boasting a 92% general accuracy rating for its core AI generation service. Unfortunately, that remaining 8% failure rate proved genuinely catastrophic. Within two days of public launch, disturbing screenshots of deeply inappropriate generated content went highly viral across the internet, and the application was permanently banned from both major mobile app stores due to strict compliance violations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Safety systems for image generation should use layered controls and human-review paths for edge cases, because a single weak classifier can miss harmful content and create major moderation and compliance risk.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced safety incident with precise accuracy and outcome claims.

claims.claim_text: The LoRA paper was submitted on June 17, 2021 and reported roughly 10,000x fewer trainable parameters and 3x lower GPU memory than full fine-tuning of GPT-3 175B.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 1
claims.rationale: These exact figures come from the LoRA paper's abstract and metadata.

claims.claim_text: QLoRA can fine-tune a 65B model on a single 48GB GPU using NF4.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 2
claims.rationale: The QLoRA paper directly makes this claim.

claims.claim_text: Nested quantization saves an additional 0.4 bits per parameter.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 3
claims.rationale: The bitsandbytes documentation explicitly states the 0.4 bits/parameter figure.

claims.claim_text: PEFT moved through the 0.18.x line and into 0.19.x.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 4
claims.rationale: The GitHub releases page directly shows the 0.18.x and 0.19.x release sequence.

claims.claim_text: DDPM requires 1000 sequential steps while DDIM or DPM-Solver can preserve acceptable quality with far fewer steps.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, DDPM scheduler row
claims.anchor_text: | **Using DDPM Scheduler in Production** | DDPM requires 1000 sequential steps for generation, leading to massive latency constraints during API inference. | Use `DDIMScheduler` or `DPMSolverMultistepScheduler` to achieve the same visual fidelity in 20-50 steps. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Using DDPM Scheduler in Production** | DDPM usually needs many more denoising steps than production-oriented schedulers, which increases latency. | Use `DDIMScheduler` or `DPMSolverMultistepScheduler` and validate the smallest step count that preserves acceptable quality for your model. |
claims.rationale: The production lesson is sound, but the exact quality-equivalence range is model- and setup-dependent.

claims.claim_text: Stable Diffusion v1.5 is a 512x512-resolution checkpoint.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, wrong resolutions row
claims.rationale: The Diffusers Stable Diffusion pipeline docs directly list the v1.5 checkpoint as 512x512 resolution.

claims.claim_text: SDXL defaults to 1024x1024 for best text-to-image results.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, wrong resolutions row
claims.rationale: The SDXL docs explicitly state 1024x1024 as the default/best text-to-image resolution.

claims.claim_text: PEFT recommends QLoRA-style targeting of all linear modules via `target_modules="all-linear"`.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, targeting only attention matrices row
claims.rationale: The PEFT LoRA developer guide explicitly recommends `target_modules="all-linear"` for QLoRA-style training.

claims.claim_text: bitsandbytes 8-bit and 4-bit training only supports training extra parameters, not the quantized base weights.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, using 4-bit training on base weights row
claims.rationale: The bitsandbytes/Transformers quantization docs state this restriction explicitly.

further_reading.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for LoRA's parameter-efficiency claims and core mechanism.

further_reading.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for 4-bit NF4 fine-tuning, single-48GB-GPU claims, and memory-saving techniques.

further_reading.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for latent diffusion, cross-attention conditioning, and the Stable Diffusion architectural foundation.

further_reading.title: Classifier-Free Diffusion Guidance
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for CFG training and inference behavior used throughout modern text-to-image pipelines.

notes: Excluded code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz items, and exercise-step prose per the pipeline rules. Surfaced table rows where they carry pricing/statistic claims; many of those rows need qualitative rewrites or fresh sourced market data rather than inline citations.
